answer: Multi-provider resilience: (1) provider abstraction layer — standardize on a common interface (LiteLLM) that supports multiple providers; (2) fallback chain — primary (GPT-4o) → secondary (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) → tertiary (Gemini Pro); (3) circuit breaker per provider — detect outage quickly, fail fast to fallback; (4) capability mapping — not all providers support the same features (function calling, JSON mode); fallback must handle capability gaps; (5) graceful degradation — if all providers fail, return partial results with clear uncertainty signals rather than hard failure; (6) user communication — surface provider status to users during degraded operation.
explanation: LLM provider outages are rare but impactful. The fallback chain must be tested regularly — don't discover capability gaps during an actual outage. LiteLLM provides a unified interface across 100+ providers, making provider switching transparent to the agent code. The capability mapping is critical: if your primary provider supports parallel function calling but your fallback doesn't, the agent must adapt its behavior.
followUpQuestions: How do you test your fallback chain without waiting for an actual provider outage?
What is the quality degradation you accept when falling back to a less capable model?
realWorldContext: A 24/7 customer service agent that must maintain service continuity during OpenAI outages that historically last 30-120 minutes.

question: Your production agent's cost per task has increased 3x over the past month without any code changes. How do you diagnose and fix this?
answer: Diagnosis: (1) break down cost by component — LLM tokens (input/output), tool calls, memory operations; (2) compare token usage per task over time — identify which component grew; (3) check for context window bloat — are prompts growing due to accumulated history or retrieved content?; (4) check tool call frequency — are agents making more tool calls per task?; (5) check task complexity distribution — has the mix of task types shifted toward more complex tasks? Fix based on root cause: context compression for bloat, tool call optimization for frequency, model routing for complexity shift.
explanation: Cost increases without code changes usually indicate data drift — the distribution of inputs has changed. Common causes: users asking more complex questions, documents in the knowledge base growing longer, or a new user segment with different behavior. The diagnosis must be data-driven: compare token usage distributions before and after the cost increase. Prompt token growth is the most common culprit — retrieved context chunks growing as the knowledge base expands.
followUpQuestions: How do you set up cost anomaly detection to catch this earlier?
What is your process for communicating cost increases to business stakeholders?
realWorldContext: A SaaS AI assistant where a 3x cost increase threatens unit economics and requires immediate diagnosis before the next billing cycle.

question: How do you implement rate limiting and backpressure for an agentic system under high load to prevent cascade failures?
answer: Rate limiting layers: (1) API gateway — rate limit incoming task requests per user/tenant using token bucket; (2) LLM call rate limiting — respect provider rate limits (TPM, RPM) using a rate limiter with queue; (3) tool call rate limiting — per-tool limits to protect downstream services; (4) backpressure — when queue depth exceeds threshold, return 429 with retry-after header rather than queuing indefinitely; (5) priority queuing — high-priority tasks (P0 incidents) bypass rate limits; (6) load shedding — drop lowest-priority tasks under extreme load rather than degrading all tasks equally.
explanation: Agentic systems amplify load — one user request can generate 10-50 LLM calls and 20-100 tool calls. Without rate limiting, a traffic spike causes cascade failures across all downstream services. The backpressure mechanism is critical: it's better to reject requests early with a clear error than to accept them and fail slowly. Priority queuing ensures critical workflows complete even under load.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement fair queuing across tenants with different SLA tiers?
What is your strategy for communicating rate limit status to agent orchestrators?
realWorldContext: An enterprise AI platform where a single large customer's batch job could exhaust LLM rate limits and degrade service for all other customers.

question: What is RAG fusion and how does it improve retrieval quality over standard RAG?
answer: RAG Fusion generates multiple query variants from the original query using an LLM, retrieves results for each variant independently, then merges and reranks all results using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF score = sum(1 / (k + rank_i)) across all query variants. This improves recall by covering different phrasings and perspectives of the information need, and improves precision by promoting documents that rank highly across multiple queries.
explanation: A single query may miss relevant documents due to vocabulary mismatch or narrow framing. RAG Fusion's multi-query approach is analogous to ensemble methods in ML — diversity in queries leads to more robust retrieval. The RRF algorithm is parameter-free and robust to score scale differences between retrieval runs. The cost is N additional retrieval calls (typically 3-5 query variants), which adds ~100-200ms latency.
followUpQuestions: How do you generate diverse query variants without them becoming too similar to each other?
What is the optimal number of query variants for RAG Fusion before diminishing returns?
realWorldContext: A technical documentation search system where engineers phrase the same question in many different ways depending on their background.

question: How do you implement self-RAG, where the agent decides when to retrieve and whether retrieved content is relevant?
answer: Self-RAG adds retrieval decision tokens to the generation process: (1) Retrieve token — model decides whether retrieval is needed for the current generation step; (2) ISREL token — model assesses whether retrieved documents are relevant; (3) ISSUP token — model assesses whether its generation is supported by retrieved content; (4) ISUSE token — model assesses whether the response is useful. These tokens enable selective retrieval (only retrieve when needed) and self-critique of generation quality. Implement by fine-tuning on Self-RAG training data or by prompting a capable model to perform these assessments.
explanation: Standard RAG always retrieves, even for questions the model can answer from parametric knowledge. Self-RAG reduces unnecessary retrieval calls (cost and latency savings) while improving quality by explicitly assessing relevance and support. The ISSUP token is particularly valuable — it enables the model to flag when its answer is not well-supported by retrieved content, triggering a retry or escalation.
followUpQuestions: How do you calibrate the retrieval decision threshold to balance cost savings vs. quality?
How does Self-RAG perform on tasks requiring multi-hop reasoning across multiple documents?
realWorldContext: A general-purpose enterprise assistant where 40% of queries can be answered from model knowledge, making selective retrieval a significant cost optimization.

question: Design an agentic system for automated security vulnerability triage that processes 1000 CVEs per day. What is the architecture?
answer: Architecture: (1) Ingestion agent: monitors NVD, vendor advisories, threat intel feeds; normalizes CVE data to canonical schema; (2) Enrichment agent: retrieves exploit availability (ExploitDB), EPSS score, affected asset inventory from CMDB; (3) Triage agent: scores CVE by risk = f(CVSS, EPSS, asset criticality, exploit availability, exposure); (4) Prioritization agent: ranks CVEs, generates remediation recommendations, assigns to teams; (5) Notification agent: sends prioritized alerts via Jira/Slack/email. RAG: vector store of past CVE resolutions for similar vulnerability pattern matching. Throughput: async pipeline with parallel enrichment, target <5 min triage per CVE.
explanation: Security triage is a high-volume, time-sensitive workflow where agent errors have real consequences. The enrichment step is the most expensive (multiple API calls per CVE) — parallelize across CVEs and cache enrichment data. The triage agent's scoring model must be explainable — security teams need to understand why a CVE was prioritized. Store triage decisions with full reasoning for audit.
followUpQuestions: How do you handle false positives where the agent incorrectly deprioritizes a critical CVE?
How do you keep the asset inventory in the CMDB current enough for accurate exposure assessment?
realWorldContext: A Fortune 100 company's security operations center processing 1000+ CVEs daily across a 50,000-asset infrastructure.

question: How do you implement streaming responses in an agentic system to improve perceived latency for end users?
answer: Streaming strategy: (1) stream final LLM generation token-by-token to the user while intermediate steps (tool calls, retrieval) run synchronously; (2) for multi-step agents, stream status updates ('Searching knowledge base...', 'Analyzing results...') between steps; (3) use Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSockets for the streaming transport; (4) implement a streaming proxy that buffers tool call results and streams the final generation; (5) handle stream interruption gracefully — if user cancels, stop all in-flight tool calls and LLM calls to avoid wasted cost.
explanation: Users perceive streaming responses as faster even when total latency is the same. The key is starting to stream as soon as possible. For ReAct agents, the final answer generation can start streaming immediately once the agent decides to answer. Status updates during tool execution reduce perceived wait time. Stream cancellation is important for cost control — a cancelled stream should not continue generating tokens.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement streaming for a multi-agent workflow where the final response depends on multiple sub-agents?
How do you handle streaming in a RAG pipeline where retrieval must complete before generation starts?
realWorldContext: A coding assistant where users expect immediate feedback — streaming the response as it generates feels 3x faster than waiting for the complete response.

question: What is the difference between fine-tuning and prompt engineering for improving agent behavior? When do you choose each?
answer: Prompt engineering: modify system prompt, few-shot examples, or instruction format — fast iteration (minutes), no training cost, but limited by model's base capabilities and context window. Fine-tuning: train the model on task-specific data — slower iteration (hours/days), training cost, but can instill behaviors that are hard to prompt (consistent output format, domain-specific reasoning patterns, reduced hallucination on specific topics). Choose prompt engineering first; fine-tune when: (1) prompt is too long/expensive, (2) behavior is inconsistent despite good prompts, (3) you need sub-100ms latency with a smaller model.
explanation: Fine-tuning is often over-applied. Most agent behavior improvements can be achieved through better prompts, few-shot examples, and retrieval. Fine-tuning is the right choice when you have 1000+ high-quality examples of the desired behavior and prompt engineering has plateaued. For tool use and function calling, fine-tuning on tool call examples can dramatically improve reliability with smaller, cheaper models.
followUpQuestions: How do you create a high-quality fine-tuning dataset for an agent task?
What is the risk of catastrophic forgetting when fine-tuning for a specific agent task?
realWorldContext: A customer service agent where fine-tuning on 10K historical conversations reduces hallucination rate from 8% to 1% compared to prompt engineering alone.

question: You are asked to build an agentic system that autonomously manages cloud infrastructure. What safety guardrails do you implement before going to production?
answer: Safety guardrails: (1) action classification — categorize all actions by reversibility (read-only, reversible write, irreversible write) and blast radius (single resource, service, region, account); (2) dry-run mode — all actions execute in dry-run first, showing what would change; (3) approval gates — irreversible or high-blast-radius actions require human approval; (4) blast radius limits — agent cannot affect more than N resources in a single task; (5) rollback capability — all changes are tracked with rollback procedures; (6) change windows — destructive actions only during approved maintenance windows; (7) anomaly detection — alert if agent attempts unusual action sequences.
explanation: Cloud infrastructure agents have the highest potential blast radius of any agentic system. The safety architecture must be defense-in-depth: multiple independent layers that each prevent catastrophic mistakes. The dry-run pattern is particularly valuable — it lets the agent show its work before executing, enabling human review without blocking the happy path. Start with read-only agents, add write capabilities incrementally as trust is established.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement rollback for infrastructure changes that have downstream dependencies?
What is your incident response plan for an agent that accidentally deletes a production database?
realWorldContext: An autonomous cloud cost optimization agent that rightsizes EC2 instances — a misconfiguration could take down production services.

question: How do you implement multi-modal tool use in an agent that processes both text and images?
answer: Multi-modal tool architecture: (1) vision tools — screenshot analysis, diagram interpretation, OCR, image search; (2) tool input schemas that accept image URLs or base64-encoded images alongside text; (3) model selection — use vision-capable models (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) for steps requiring image understanding; (4) image preprocessing — resize, compress, and format images before passing to model to minimize token cost; (5) result grounding — when agent reasons about an image, require it to cite specific visual elements; (6) fallback — if vision model unavailable, use OCR to extract text and fall back to text-only processing.
explanation: Multi-modal agents unlock use cases impossible with text-only: UI automation (screenshot → action), document processing (scan → extract), and visual QA. The key engineering challenge is image token cost — a 1024x1024 image costs ~1700 tokens in GPT-4o. Resize images to the minimum resolution needed for the task. For UI automation, use accessibility trees (text representation of UI) instead of screenshots where possible — much cheaper and more reliable.
followUpQuestions: How do you handle multi-modal tool use in a streaming response pipeline?
What are the privacy implications of passing user screenshots to external LLM APIs?
realWorldContext: A UI automation agent that interprets screenshots to navigate web applications for automated testing and RPA workflows.

question: As a principal AI architect, how do you define and communicate the boundaries of what an agentic system should and should not do autonomously?
answer: Framework for autonomy boundaries: (1) reversibility matrix — map all agent actions to reversible/irreversible and define autonomy level for each; (2) confidence thresholds — agent acts autonomously above threshold, escalates below; (3) domain boundaries — explicit list of domains where agent has authority (e.g., read-only data access, draft communications) vs. requires human (financial transactions, external communications); (4) stakeholder alignment — document boundaries in a 'agent charter' reviewed by legal, compliance, and business owners; (5) progressive autonomy — start narrow, expand boundaries as trust is established through track record.
explanation: Autonomy boundaries are as much a governance and communication challenge as a technical one. The agent charter is a powerful artifact — it creates shared understanding between engineering, business, and compliance about what the agent will and won't do. Progressive autonomy is the right deployment strategy: demonstrate reliability in a constrained scope before expanding. This builds organizational trust and catches failure modes before they have large impact.
followUpQuestions: How do you handle situations where the agent encounters a task that falls outside its defined boundaries?
How do you update autonomy boundaries as the agent's track record improves?
realWorldContext: Defining the autonomy scope for an AI procurement agent at a Fortune 500 company with strict financial controls and approval workflows.

question: How do you implement agent-to-agent communication using the Model Context Protocol (MCP)? What are its advantages over custom APIs?
answer: MCP (Anthropic's Model Context Protocol) standardizes how agents expose tools, resources, and prompts to other agents and LLMs. Implementation: (1) MCP server — expose tools as MCP-compliant endpoints with standardized schemas; (2) MCP client — agent discovers and calls tools via MCP protocol; (3) resource exposure — agents can expose data sources (files, databases) as MCP resources; (4) prompt templates — share reusable prompt components via MCP. Advantages over custom APIs: standardized discovery, no custom integration code per tool, ecosystem compatibility (any MCP-compatible client works), built-in schema validation.
explanation: MCP is emerging as the USB-C of AI tool integration — a standard interface that eliminates the N×M integration problem. Without MCP, each agent-tool pair requires custom integration code. With MCP, any MCP-compatible agent can use any MCP-compatible tool. The ecosystem is growing rapidly (Claude Desktop, Cursor, and many others support MCP). For enterprise deployments, MCP simplifies tool governance — all tools are registered in a central MCP registry.
followUpQuestions: How do you implement authentication and authorization in an MCP server?
What are the current limitations of MCP that prevent full adoption in enterprise settings?
realWorldContext: An enterprise AI platform standardizing on MCP to allow any internal agent to use any approved tool without custom integration work.

question: Design the architecture for an agentic AI platform that will serve as the foundation for 50 different enterprise use cases. What are the core platform capabilities?
answer: Platform architecture: (1) Agent runtime — execution engine supporting ReAct, Plan-Execute, and custom graph-based workflows; (2) Tool registry — centralized catalog with dynamic retrieval, versioning, and permission management; (3) Memory service — unified API for episodic, semantic, and working memory across all agents; (4) Model gateway — multi-provider LLM routing with cost tracking, rate limiting, and fallback; (5) Observability — distributed tracing, metrics, and structured logging for all agent executions; (6) Evaluation framework — automated testing, golden datasets, and continuous quality monitoring; (7) Governance layer — RBAC, audit logging, human-in-the-loop workflows, and compliance reporting; (8) Developer experience — SDK, local development tools, and deployment pipeline.
explanation: A platform approach amortizes the cost of building production-grade infrastructure across all use cases. The key insight is that 80% of what makes an agent production-ready (observability, security, reliability) is the same across all use cases. The platform provides these as shared services; use-case teams focus on agent logic and domain knowledge. The developer experience is critical for adoption — if the platform is hard to use, teams will build their own solutions.
followUpQuestions: How do you balance platform standardization with the flexibility needed for diverse use cases?
What is your strategy for migrating existing agents to the platform without disrupting production?
realWorldContext: A large enterprise building a centralized AI platform to support 50 business units deploying agents for sales, operations, finance, and HR use cases.
